w:document.mc:Ignorable: w14 w15 w16se w16cid w16 w16cex w16sdtdh w16sdtfl w16du wp14

w:r.w:t: is a comprehensive web-based application developed for Shobhit University, Gangoh. This system is designed to streamline the process of managing backlog carryover requests for students who have failed to clear certain subjects in their regular examinations. The portal provides a digital platform where students can submit their carryover applications and administrators can efficiently process, approve, or reject these requests.

Traditional methods of handling carryover requests involve manual paperwork, which is time-consuming, error-prone, and difficult to track. Students often face challenges in submitting their applications, and administrators struggle with managing large volumes of requests. The lack of a centralized system leads to delays, miscommunication, and inefficiency in the overall process.
To digitize the carryover request submission process for students
To provide an efficient admin dashboard for managing and processing requests
To implement real-time status tracking for submitted requests
To reduce manual paperwork and administrative overhead
To improve transparency and communication between students and administrators
To maintain accurate records of all carryover requests and their statuses
To provide data export capabilities for reporting and analysis

The scope of this project encompasses the development of a full-stack web application with the following components: a student-facing portal for submitting carryover requests, an admin dashboard for managing and processing these requests, a robust backend API for handling business logic and data persistence, and a MySQL database for storing all application data. The system supports multiple courses including B.Tech, M.Tech, BCA, and MCA with their respective semester structures.

is a multi-tier web application that follows the Model-View-Controller (MVC) architectural pattern. The system consists of three main layers: the presentation layer (frontend), the application layer (backend API), and the data layer (database). This separation of concerns ensures maintainability, scalability, and testability of the application.
Students can submit carryover requests by providing their personal and academic details. The system validates student information against a pre-existing database of enrolled students.
The student form dynamically adjusts based on the selected course, showing appropriate semester options and automatically calculating year and semester type.
A comprehensive dashboard provides administrators with real-time statistics including total requests, pending requests, accepted requests, and rejected requests.
Administrators can approve, reject, or move requests to trash. The system supports bulk operations for efficient processing.
Advanced search and filter functionality allows administrators to quickly find specific requests based on various criteria including course, year, semester, and status.
The system supports exporting request data to Excel format for reporting and analysis purposes.
The application is fully responsive and works seamlessly on desktop, tablet, and mobile devices.
The system defines two primary user roles:

Students are the primary users who submit carryover requests. They can access the student portal to fill out the carryover form, provide their personal and academic details, select the subjects they need to clear, and submit their applications. Students can track the status of their requests through the system.

Administrators have full access to the admin dashboard where they can view all submitted requests, approve or reject requests, manage the request lifecycle, export data, and monitor system statistics. Administrators are authenticated using hardcoded credentials for security purposes.

follows a three-tier architecture pattern that separates the application into distinct layers: the presentation layer, the business logic layer, and the data access layer. This architecture promotes separation of concerns, making the system easier to maintain, test, and scale.
The system consists of the following major components:
Built using HTML5, CSS3, and JavaScript, the frontend provides the user interface for both students and administrators. It includes responsive design elements and modern UI components.

Developed using Python Flask framework, the backend handles all business logic, API endpoints, authentication, and data processing. It serves as the intermediary between the frontend and the database.

MySQL database stores all application data including student information, carryover subjects, requests, and their statuses. The database is designed with proper normalization and indexing for optimal performance.
RESTful API endpoints facilitate communication between the frontend and backend. The API follows standard HTTP methods and JSON data format for request and response handling.

follows a standard request-response cycle. When a student submits a carryover request, the frontend sends an HTTP POST request to the backend API. The backend validates the data, processes the request, and stores the information in the database. For admin operations, the frontend sends requests to specific API endpoints, which execute the required business logic and return the results to the frontend for display.
The system architecture can be visualized as follows:
is built using the following technologies:
The latest version of HyperText Markup Language is used for structuring the web pages. HTML5 provides semantic elements, improved form controls, and better support for multimedia content.

Cascading Style Sheets version 3 is used for styling the application. CSS3 features including flexbox, grid, animations, transitions, and media queries are utilized to create a modern and responsive user interface.

Modern JavaScript with ES6+ features is used for client-side interactivity, form validation, API communication, and dynamic content generation. Async/await pattern is used for handling asynchronous operations.
A popular icon library providing scalable vector icons that can be customized with CSS. Icons are used throughout the application for better visual communication.
The Poppins font family is used for typography, providing a clean and modern look to the application.
The backend is built using the following technologies:
The primary programming language for the backend. Python provides excellent libraries for web development, data processing, and database interaction.
A lightweight WSGI web application framework in Python. Flask is chosen for its simplicity, flexibility, and extensive ecosystem of extensions.
Extension for handling Cross-Origin Resource Sharing (CORS), enabling the frontend to communicate with the backend API from different origins.
The official MySQL driver for Python, providing efficient and secure database connectivity.
A powerful data manipulation library used for processing and exporting data to Excel format.
A library for reading and writing Excel files, used in conjunction with Pandas for data export functionality.

MySQL is used as the relational database management system (RDBMS) for storing all application data. MySQL is chosen for its reliability, performance, and widespread adoption in web applications. The database uses the InnoDB storage engine for ACID compliance and foreign key support.
Containerization platform used for packaging the application and its dependencies into a portable container for consistent deployment across different environments.
Version control system for tracking changes in the source code and collaborating with team members.
Integrated development environment (IDE) used for writing, debugging, and testing the application code.

uses a relational database named "carryoverportal" with four main tables: students, subjects, requests, and request_subjects. The database is designed with proper normalization to minimize data redundancy and ensure data integrity. Foreign key constraints are used to maintain referential integrity between related tables.
The students table stores information about enrolled students. Each student is identified by a unique roll number, which serves as the primary key. The table includes the following fields:
roll_number (VARCHAR(20), PRIMARY KEY): Unique identifier for each student
enrollment_no (VARCHAR(50), NOT NULL): Student enrollment number
name (VARCHAR(255), NOT NULL): Full name of the student
father_name (VARCHAR(255), NOT NULL): Father's name of the student
contact (VARCHAR(15), NOT NULL): Contact phone number
course (VARCHAR(50), NOT NULL): Course enrolled (B.Tech, M.Tech, BCA, MCA)
semester_type (VARCHAR(10), NOT NULL): Odd or Even semester
The subjects table stores information about carryover subjects for each student. A student can have multiple carryover subjects. The composite primary key consists of roll_number and subject_code.
roll_number (VARCHAR(20), NOT NULL, FOREIGN KEY): References students table
subject_code (VARCHAR(50), NOT NULL): Unique code for the subject
subject_name (VARCHAR(255), NOT NULL): Name of the subject
The requests table stores carryover request information. Each request is associated with a student and has a unique request number. The table tracks the overall status of the request.
roll_number (VARCHAR(20), NOT NULL, FOREIGN KEY): References students table
request_number (VARCHAR(50), NOT NULL): Unique identifier for the request
status (VARCHAR(20), NOT NULL, DEFAULT 'pending'): Request status (pending, accepted, rejected, trashed)
amount (DECIMAL(10,2), DEFAULT 0): Fee amount for the request
rejection_reason (TEXT): Reason for rejection if applicable
The request_subjects table links requests to specific subjects. This junction table allows tracking the status of each subject within a request independently.
roll_number (VARCHAR(20), NOT NULL, FOREIGN KEY): References students table
request_number (VARCHAR(50), NOT NULL, FOREIGN KEY): References requests table
subject_code (VARCHAR(50), NOT NULL, FOREIGN KEY): References subjects table
status (VARCHAR(20), NOT NULL, DEFAULT 'pending'): Subject request status
rejection_reason (TEXT): Reason for rejection if applicable
Indexes are created on frequently queried columns to improve query performance. The following indexes are implemented:

A database view named v_dashboard_stats is created to provide aggregated statistics for the admin dashboard. This view calculates the total number of requests and categorizes them by status (pending, accepted, rejected, trashed) for efficient dashboard rendering.
The backend is built using Flask, a lightweight Python web framework. The application is configured with the following components:
Flask application instance with static file serving from the current directory
CORS enabled for all routes to support cross-origin requests from the frontend
Session management using Flask's built-in session support with a secret key
Database initialization function to create tables if they don't exist

The database connection is managed through a dedicated function get_db_connection() that creates and returns a MySQL connection object. The connection configuration includes host, port, username, password, and database name. Error handling is implemented to catch and log connection failures.
The backend provides the following RESTful API endpoints:
: Authenticates admin users with username and password. Returns success message and creates a session upon successful authentication.
: Logs out the admin user by clearing the session data.
: Checks if the current user is authenticated by verifying the session.
: Returns dashboard statistics including total requests, pending requests, accepted requests, and rejected requests.
: Retrieves all requests with optional status filter. Supports query parameters for filtering by status and limiting results.
: Approves a specific request for a given subject. Updates the status to "accepted" in the request_subjects table.
: Rejects a specific request for a given subject. Updates the status to "rejected" and stores the rejection reason.
: Moves selected requests to trash by updating their status to "trashed". Supports bulk operations.
: Restores requests from trash to pending status. Supports bulk operations.
: Permanently deletes selected request subjects from the database. This operation cannot be undone.
: Accepts student carryover form submissions. Validates the data, creates student record if not exists, creates request record, and stores subject information.

The backend implements comprehensive error handling to ensure robust operation. Database errors are caught and appropriate error messages are returned to the client. Authentication errors return 401 Unauthorized status codes. Validation errors return 400 Bad Request status codes with descriptive error messages.

User sessions are managed using Flask's built-in session support. A secret key is generated using the secrets module for secure session management. The session stores the admin login status and username. Session data is cleared upon logout to ensure security.

The frontend consists of multiple HTML pages, each serving a specific purpose in the application. The HTML structure follows semantic markup practices with proper use of header, main, section, and footer elements. Form elements use appropriate input types and validation attributes.
The application uses two CSS files: style.css for the student portal and styles.css for the admin dashboard. The styling implements a modern, professional design with the following features:
CSS custom properties (variables) for consistent theming
Flexbox and CSS Grid for responsive layouts
Smooth transitions and animations for enhanced user experience
Gradient backgrounds and shadow effects for depth
Responsive design with media queries for mobile, tablet, and desktop
Custom form controls and input styling
Table styling with hover effects and alternating row colors
Button styles with hover and active states
Modal and overlay styling for dialogs
JavaScript is used extensively for client-side interactivity and API communication. The main JavaScript files are:

This file contains core application functions including admin login handling, form submission logic, and utility functions. It implements the fetch API for making HTTP requests to the backend and handles responses appropriately.

This file handles student form functionality including dynamic subject addition and removal, custom dropdown initialization, course-semester dynamic dropdown, roll number auto-fetch, form validation, and submission handling.
This file contains a JavaScript object mapping roll numbers to student information. It is used for auto-filling student details when a valid roll number is entered in the form.

Client-side form validation is implemented to provide immediate feedback to users. The validation includes checking for required fields, validating input formats (phone numbers, roll numbers), and ensuring data consistency. Server-side validation is also performed for security.

The frontend communicates with the backend using the Fetch API. All API calls are asynchronous using async/await syntax for better readability. Error handling is implemented to display appropriate messages to users when API calls fail. Loading indicators are shown during API operations to improve user experience.
The landing page serves as the entry point for the

. It displays the Shobhit University logo, the portal title, and two navigation buttons: one for students and one for teachers (administrators). The page features a modern gradient background with animated elements for visual appeal.
The student form page is where students submit their carryover requests. The form is divided into three main sections:
This section collects personal information about the student:
Roll Number: Unique identifier that auto-fills other fields when valid
Student Name: Full name of the student
Father's Name: Father's name of the student
Course: Selection from B.Tech, M.Tech, BCA, or MCA
Semester: Dynamic dropdown based on selected course
Year: Auto-calculated based on semester selection
Semester Type: Auto-determined as Odd or Even based on semester

This section allows students to add multiple carryover subjects. Each subject entry includes subject name and subject code fields. Students can add up to 10 subjects and remove subjects as needed. The form dynamically updates the subject count and total fee calculation.

When a student enters their roll number, the system automatically searches the student database and fills in the enrollment number, student name, father's name, and course if a matching record is found. This feature reduces data entry errors and improves the
user experience. Visual feedback is provided to indicate whether the roll number was found or not.

The semester dropdown dynamically populates based on the selected course. Each course has a specific number of semesters: B.Tech has 8 semesters, M.Tech has 4 semesters, BCA has 6 semesters, and MCA has 4 semesters. When a semester is selected, the year and semester type are automatically calculated and displayed.
When the student submits the form, the following process occurs:
1. Client-side validation checks all required fields
2. Student data is validated against the student database
3. Form data is collected and formatted for API submission
4. An HTTP POST request is sent to the /api/submit-form endpoint
5. The backend validates the data and creates database records
6. A success message is displayed to the student
7. The form is reset for potential additional submissions

The admin dashboard is a comprehensive interface for managing carryover requests. It provides administrators with real-time statistics, request management capabilities, and data export functionality. The dashboard is organized into multiple sections accessible via a sidebar navigation.
The main dashboard section displays four key statistics cards:
1. Total Requests: Shows the total number of carryover requests in the system
2. Pending Requests: Displays the count of requests awaiting review
3. Accepted Requests: Shows the number of approved requests
4. Rejected Requests: Displays the count of rejected requests
These statistics are fetched from the backend API and updated in real-time when the dashboard loads.
The dashboard includes the following request management sections:

This section displays all carryover requests in a tabular format. The table includes columns for roll number, student name, course, year, subject code, subject name, status, and action buttons. Administrators can approve, reject, or move requests to trash from this section.
This section shows only requests that have been approved. Administrators can view details of accepted requests and move them to trash if needed.
This section displays requests that have been rejected along with their rejection reasons. Administrators can review rejection reasons and take appropriate actions.
This section shows requests that are awaiting review. Administrators can approve or reject these requests directly from this section.
This section contains requests that have been moved to trash. Administrators can restore these requests to pending status or permanently delete them.
The admin dashboard includes advanced search and filter capabilities:
1. Search Box: Allows searching by roll number, student name, or subject code
2. Course Filter: Filter requests by course (B.Tech, M.Tech, BCA, MCA)
3. Year Filter: Filter by academic year (1st, 2nd, 3rd, 4th Year)
4. Semester Type Filter: Filter by Odd or Even semester
5. Semester Filter: Dynamic filter based on selected course
Filters can be combined for precise results. The filter state is managed client-side for immediate feedback.
Administrators can perform bulk operations on multiple requests simultaneously:
1. Bulk Approve: Approve multiple pending requests at once
2. Bulk Reject: Reject multiple requests with a common rejection reason
3. Bulk Move to Trash: Move multiple requests to trash
4. Bulk Restore: Restore multiple requests from trash to pending
5. Bulk Delete: Permanently delete multiple requests
These operations significantly improve administrative efficiency when handling large volumes of requests.

exposes a RESTful API for communication between the frontend and backend. All API endpoints return JSON responses and accept JSON request bodies. The API uses standard HTTP status codes to indicate success or failure of requests.
Authenticates admin users with username and password
Logs out the admin user by clearing the session
Returns dashboard statistics including request counts by status
Retrieves all requests with optional filtering
status (optional): Filter by status (pending, accepted, rejected, trashed)
limit (optional): Limit number of results returned
Approves a specific request for a given subject
Rejects a specific request for a given subject
The admin authentication system uses hardcoded credentials stored in the backend code. While this approach is simple, it provides basic security for the application. The credentials are:
For production deployment, it is recommended to implement a more robust authentication system with password hashing, user management, and role-based access control.

User sessions are managed using Flask's built-in session support. A secret key is generated using the secrets module with 32 bytes of randomness for secure session management. Session data is stored server-side and a session cookie is sent to the client. The session is cleared upon logout to prevent session hijacking.

The application uses parameterized queries for all database operations to prevent SQL injection attacks. User input is never directly concatenated into SQL queries. Instead, placeholders (%s) are used and values are passed as separate parameters to the cursor.execute() method.

Cross-Origin Resource Sharing (CORS) is enabled for all routes using the Flask-CORS extension. This allows the frontend to communicate with the backend API from different origins. For production deployment, it is recommended to configure CORS to allow only specific origins.

Both client-side and server-side input validation are implemented. Client-side validation provides immediate feedback to users, while server-side validation ensures data integrity and security. Required fields are validated, input formats are checked, and data types are verified before processing.

Comprehensive error handling is implemented throughout the application. Database errors are caught and appropriate error messages are returned to the client without exposing sensitive information. Authentication errors return 401 Unauthorized status codes. Validation errors return 400 Bad Request status codes with descriptive error messages.
1. Simplicity: Clean and uncluttered interface with clear visual hierarchy
2. Consistency: Uniform design elements and interactions throughout the application
3. Accessibility: Proper color contrast, readable fonts, and keyboard navigation support
4. Responsiveness: Adaptive layout that works on all screen sizes
5. Feedback: Visual feedback for user actions and system status
6. Efficiency: Streamlined workflows for common tasks
The application uses a professional color scheme with the following primary colors:
1. Primary Blue (#3b82f6): Used for primary actions and highlights
2. Dark Blue (#1e3c72): Used for backgrounds and headers
3. Green (#10b981): Used for success states and positive actions
4. Red (#ef4444): Used for error states and destructive actions
5. Gray (#6b7280): Used for secondary text and borders
6. White (#ffffff): Used for backgrounds and content areas

The Poppins font family is used throughout the application for a modern and readable appearance. Different font weights (300, 400, 500, 600, 700) are used to create visual hierarchy and emphasis. Font sizes are carefully chosen to ensure readability on all devices.
Font Awesome icons are used throughout the application for visual communication. Icons are chosen to be intuitive and consistent with common web conventions. Examples include:
1. User icons for student and admin roles
CSS animations and transitions are used to enhance the user experience:
1. Fade-in animations for page elements on load
These animations are subtle and purposeful, improving usability without being distracting.

follows a mobile-first design approach, where the base styles are designed for mobile devices and then enhanced for larger screens using media queries. This ensures that the application works well on all devices, from smartphones to desktop computers.
The following breakpoints are used for responsive design:
1024px: Small desktops and tablets in landscape mode
The sidebar navigation collapses into a hamburger menu on mobile devices. The menu can be toggled by clicking the menu button. On desktop, the sidebar is always visible.

Form layouts adapt to screen size. On mobile, form fields stack vertically. On desktop, fields are arranged in a grid layout. Input sizes and button dimensions adjust for touch-friendly interaction on mobile devices.
Data tables are responsive and scroll horizontally on small screens. Table cells have minimum heights for touch-friendly interaction. Action buttons are sized appropriately for mobile devices.
Statistics cards stack vertically on mobile and arrange in a grid on desktop. Card padding and font sizes adjust for different screen sizes.
The application includes specific optimizations for touch devices:
1. Minimum touch target size of 48px for interactive elements
2. Hover effects are disabled on touch devices
3. Active states provide visual feedback for touch interactions
4. Form inputs have appropriate font sizes to prevent zoom on iOS
5. Safe area support for devices with notches
follows a comprehensive testing strategy that includes:
1. Unit Testing: Testing individual functions and components
2. Integration Testing: Testing interactions between components
3. User Acceptance Testing: Testing with real users to ensure requirements are met
4. Cross-Browser Testing: Testing on different web browsers
5. Responsive Testing: Testing on different screen sizes and devices
Student form submission with valid data
Student form submission with invalid data
Dynamic semester dropdown based on course selection
Responsive design on different screen sizes
The following quality metrics are tracked:
1. Code Coverage: Percentage of code covered by tests
2. Bug Density: Number of bugs per lines of code
3. Response Time: Time taken for API responses
4. Page Load Time: Time taken for pages to load
5. User Satisfaction: Feedback from users on usability
Bugs and issues are tracked using a systematic approach. Each bug is documented with:
Bugs are prioritized based on severity and impact on the application.
can be deployed using the following methods:
For local development and testing, the application can be run directly using Python. The Flask development server is used for local deployment. The application is accessible at http://localhost:5000.
The application includes a Dockerfile for containerized deployment. Docker provides consistent deployment across different environments. The Dockerfile specifies:
1. Build the image: docker build -t carryover-portal .
2. Run the container: docker run -p 5000:5000 carryover-portal
For production deployment, the following considerations should be addressed:
1. Use a production WSGI server (Gunicorn or Waitress) instead of Flask development server
2. Configure a reverse proxy (Nginx or Apache) for better performance and security
3. Set up SSL/TLS certificates for HTTPS
4. Configure environment variables for sensitive data
5. Set up database backups and recovery procedures
7. Configure firewall rules and security groups
The application uses the following environment configuration:
6. Flask Secret Key: Auto-generated using secrets module
For production, these values should be configured using environment variables or configuration files.
The following enhancements are planned for the near future:
Send email notifications to students when their request status changes. This will improve communication and keep students informed about their applications.
Integrate SMS gateway to send text message notifications for important updates.
Integrate online payment gateway for collecting carryover fees. This will streamline the payment process and reduce manual cash handling.
Allow students to upload supporting documents such as mark sheets and identity proof.
Generate detailed reports with charts and graphs for administrative analysis.
The following enhancements are planned for the long term:
Develop native mobile applications for iOS and Android platforms to provide better accessibility and user experience.
Add support for multiple languages to cater to a diverse student population.
Implement machine learning algorithms to predict request approval rates and identify patterns in student data.
Integrate with existing university systems such as student information systems and examination management systems.
Implement blockchain technology for secure and tamper-proof verification of carryover certificates.
1. Implementing caching mechanisms for frequently accessed data
2. Optimizing database queries with better indexing
3. Implementing pagination for large data sets
5. Implementing lazy loading for images and heavy content

for Shobhit University, Gangoh is a comprehensive web-based application that successfully digitizes the process of managing backlog carryover requests. The system provides a user-friendly interface for students to submit their applications and an efficient admin dashboard for administrators to process these requests.
The project has achieved the following key objectives:
Successfully developed a full-stack web application using modern technologies
Implemented a responsive design that works on all devices
Created an intuitive student portal with dynamic form generation
Developed a comprehensive admin dashboard with advanced features
Implemented secure authentication and session management
Created a robust backend API with proper error handling
Designed a normalized database schema with proper indexing
Implemented search, filter, and bulk operation functionality
Added data export capabilities for reporting
Deployed the application using Docker for consistent environments
Throughout the development of this project, several valuable lessons were learned:
1. Importance of thorough requirements gathering before development
2. Benefits of following MVC architecture for maintainable code
3. Value of responsive design for reaching a wider audience
4. Importance of security considerations from the beginning
5. Benefits of using modern JavaScript features for cleaner code
6. Value of comprehensive testing for ensuring quality
7. Importance of documentation for future maintenance

represents a significant step forward in digitizing administrative processes at Shobhit University. The system not only improves efficiency but also enhances transparency and communication between students and
administrators. With planned future enhancements, the system has the potential to become an even more powerful tool for academic administration.
4. MDN Web Docs. (2024). HTML, CSS, and JavaScript Documentation. Retrieved from https://developer.mozilla.org/
5. Font Awesome Documentation. (2024). Retrieved from https://fontawesome.com/docs
The project consists of the following files:
index.html - Landing page with role selection
subjects - Stores carryover subjects for students
request_subjects - Links requests to specific subjects
